title.en: Azure AI Foundry: Hubs, Projects & the Enterprise AI Platform

description.en: Understand what Azure AI Foundry is, how hubs and projects organize enterprise AI work, and why the hub/project model matters for governance, cost, and team collaboration.

story_variants.en:
## Welcome to the Enterprise AI Control Plane

When a single data scientist experiments with GPT models, an API key is enough. When a 5,000-person enterprise builds forty AI applications across twelve teams, an API key becomes a liability: nobody knows who deployed what, costs blur together, and security reviews stall every launch. **Azure AI Foundry** (formerly *Azure AI Studio*, rebranded at Ignite 2024) is Microsoft's answer — a unified platform for building, evaluating, deploying, and governing generative AI applications.

> Think of Foundry as the layer between raw Azure services (OpenAI models, AI Search, storage) and your application teams. It packages models, tooling, safety, and governance into one portal, one SDK, and one resource model.

## The Two-Level Resource Model: Hubs and Projects

Foundry organizes work with two Azure resources:

- **Hub** — the *shared infrastructure* layer, owned by a platform or IT team. A hub carries the security configuration (virtual network, customer-managed keys, managed identity), shared connections (Azure OpenAI, Azure AI Search, storage accounts), and compute quota. You typically create **one hub per business unit or environment** (e.g., `hub-retail-prod`).
- **Project** — the *workspace* layer, owned by an application team. Each project lives inside a hub and **inherits** the hub's connections, network isolation, and policies. Projects hold the actual work: deployments, prompt flows, agents, evaluations, datasets, and indexes.

This split mirrors how enterprises actually operate: a central team hardens the platform once; dozens of app teams then self-serve projects without filing security tickets for each one.

| Concern | Lives on the Hub | Lives on the Project |
|---|---|---|
| Networking / private endpoints | Yes | Inherited |
| Connections (OpenAI, AI Search, APIs) | Shared connections | Project-scoped connections allowed |
| Model deployments | Shared (hub-level AOAI) | Project deployments |
| Members & RBAC roles | Hub admins, defaults | Per-project developers |
| Agents, flows, evaluations | — | Yes |
| Cost tracking | Aggregate | Tag & filter per project |

> **2025 note:** Foundry also introduced a lighter-weight *Foundry project* type built directly on an Azure AI Foundry resource (no hub required) — ideal for teams that mainly need model inference and agents. Hub-based projects remain the choice when you need Prompt Flow, managed VNet isolation, or heavy ML-style assets. Knowing *which project type* to pick is a real architectural decision in 2025-2026.

## Why Not Just Use Azure OpenAI Directly?

You can call Azure OpenAI with three lines of code. Foundry adds what enterprises are actually graded on:

- **Model catalog** — 1,900+ models (OpenAI, Meta Llama, Microsoft Phi, Mistral, Cohere, DeepSeek, Stability) behind one catalog with benchmarks and deployment options.
- **Agent Service** — managed, stateful agents with tools, file search, and code interpreter (GA in 2025).
- **Prompt Flow & tracing** — build and debug LLM pipelines visually or in code.
- **Evaluations** — built-in quality and safety evaluators (groundedness, relevance, coherence, harm categories) so *shipping* requires *measuring*.
- **Content safety** — configurable filters, prompt-shield jailbreak detection, blocklists.
- **Governance** — RBAC via Microsoft Entra ID, policy enforcement, audit trails.

## RBAC: Who Does What

Foundry uses Entra ID roles. The common pattern:

- **Owner/Contributor on the hub** — platform engineers only.
- **Azure AI Developer** on a project — app developers: can create deployments, flows, agents, indexes, but cannot change networking or hub connections.
- **Reader** — auditors and stakeholders.

> **Pitfall:** granting developers Contributor at the *hub* scope. They inherit power over every project and shared connection. Always scope developer roles to the project.

## A Realistic Setup Walkthrough

A retail company standing up Foundry might do this:

1. Platform team creates `hub-retail-prod` with a managed virtual network, customer-managed keys, and a shared Azure OpenAI connection.
2. They set a default storage account and register an Azure AI Search connection for RAG workloads.
3. The support team creates project `support-copilot`; the merchandising team creates `product-descriptions`. Both inherit networking and connections instantly.
4. Each project deploys models, builds agents, and runs evaluations independently — but cost reports and safety policies roll up to the hub.

## Common Pitfalls

- **One hub for everything.** Dev experiments and prod workloads sharing a hub means shared quota and shared blast radius. Separate hubs per environment.
- **Ignoring regions.** Model availability differs by region; your hub's region constrains which models you can deploy without cross-region setups. Check availability *before* choosing the hub region.
- **Treating projects as folders.** A project is a security and cost boundary, not a directory. Too many micro-projects fragment quota and reviews; too few blur ownership. One project per application is the sweet spot.
- **Hard-coding keys.** Foundry connections + managed identity exist precisely so your code never holds a raw API key.

## What You'll Build in This Track

Across this track you'll move through the full Foundry lifecycle: choosing models from the catalog, deploying with the right pricing model, building Prompt Flow pipelines and enterprise RAG, running managed agents, evaluating and red-teaming, then wiring tracing, cost controls, and governance for production. Every lesson uses the hub/project vocabulary you just learned — it is the coordinate system for everything else in Foundry.

description.en: The hub owns shared security and connections; projects inherit them and hold the actual AI assets.

left.label: Raw API keys per team: no audit, no shared quota, security review per app

right.label: Hub + projects: harden once, self-serve projects, roll-up governance

description.en: Why enterprises adopt the hub/project model instead of scattering API keys.

animation_sequence.code:
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

client = AIProjectClient(
    endpoint="https://myhub.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/support-copilot",
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential())

explanation.en: The projects SDK is your programmatic door into a Foundry project.
DefaultAzureCredential uses Entra ID — no API keys in code.
The client targets one specific project endpoint inside the hub.
Keyless auth means RBAC decides what this identity can touch.

description.en: The enterprise onboarding flow that hubs and projects enable.
Hubs and projects are real Azure resources you can list and manage with the CLI.

instruction.en: Simulate the Foundry hub/project inheritance model in pure Python. Implement create_hub(name, connections) returning a dict with keys 'name' and 'connections', and create_project(hub, name) returning a dict with keys 'name', 'hub', and 'connections' where the project inherits a COPY of the hub's connections list. Then create hub 'ml-hub-east' with connections ['azure-openai', 'ai-search'], create projects 'support-bot' and 'doc-summarizer' in it, and print exactly: first a line 'Hub: <name> | shared connections: <count>', then for each project a line 'Project <name> inherits: <connections joined by comma-space>'. This mirrors how real projects inherit hub connections without re-configuring security.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
Hub: ml-hub-east | shared connections: 2
Project support-bot inherits: azure-openai, ai-search
Project doc-summarizer inherits: azure-openai, ai-search

en: create_hub is a one-liner: return {'name': name, 'connections': list(connections)}
In create_project, copy with list(hub['connections']) so mutating the project never mutates the hub — that is the point of inheritance-by-copy.
Use ', '.join(p['connections']) and f-strings to match the output exactly, including the pipe and spaces in the hub line.

practice_challenges.test_code:
h = create_hub('h1', ['a', 'b'])
assert h == {'name': 'h1', 'connections': ['a', 'b']}
p = create_project(h, 'p1')
assert p['name'] == 'p1' and p['hub'] == 'h1' and p['connections'] == ['a', 'b']
p['connections'].append('c')
assert h['connections'] == ['a', 'b'], 'project must copy, not alias, hub connections'

q.en: In Azure AI Foundry, which resource owns networking, customer-managed keys, and shared connections?
What was Azure AI Foundry called before its rebrand at Ignite 2024?
A developer needs to create deployments and agents in one project but must not alter hub networking. Which role scope is correct?

en: Azure AI Developer on the project

q.en: Why is putting dev and prod workloads in one hub considered a pitfall?

en: Hubs cannot hold more than one project
They share quota and blast radius, mixing experiments with production
Projects in the same hub cannot use different models
Azure charges double for shared hubs
